summary: Spanish Baroque painter from Seville. Known for genre scenes of street children (The Young Beggar, Boys Eating Grapes and Melon, Two Women at a Window) and numerous depictions of the Immaculate Conception. Cultivated a diffuse 'vaporous style' (estilo vaporoso). Died after falling from scaffolding.

works.name: Immaculate Conception paintings
works.description: Nearly twenty versions of the Immaculate Conception, featuring Mary in a blue cloak surrounded by cherubs. The Immaculate Conception of Los Venerables was possibly the most expensive painting in the world when the Louvre bought it from Marshal Soult's estate. 'The Colossal' version unusually features a full moon at the bottom with cherubs struggling to hold up a cloud. References to Marian litanies are absent from his versions. Exemplifies his estilo vaporoso. Wikipedia

works.name: Boys Eating Grapes and Melon
works.description: Murillo's best-known genre scene. Two impoverished children (beggar boys) sumptuously devour grapes and a melon. Also known as Children Eating Grapes and a Melon.

clues.clue: Two poor children sumptuously devour grapes and melons; described as artist's best-known genre scene
examples: Two poor children (*) sumptuously devour grapes and melons in this artist's best known genre-scene.
name this Spanish Baroque painter of Boys Eating Grapes with Melon

works.name: The Young Beggar
works.description: A painting of a street urchin, one of Murillo's early tenebrist genre scenes alongside Girl of Galicia. Frequently cited alongside Boys Eating Grapes and Melon as a signature work.

clues.clue: Street urchin genre painting; one of his early tenebrist works (alongside Girl of Galicia); frequently cited as a signature genre work
examples: Name this painter also known for his depictions of children and urchins, such as The Young Beggar.
early tenebrist paintings The Young Beggar and Girl of Galicia

clues.clue: The Little Fruit Seller: another contemporary-life genre scene
examples: This artist's works depicting contemporary life include The Young Beggar and The Little Fruit Seller.

works.name: Two Women at a Window (A Girl and Her Duena)
works.description: A leering/flirtatious woman lifts her veil while a somber bespectacled woman (duena) holds the head of a boy. Also described as a woman covering her laugh with her mantilla shawl. Depicts Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid.

clues.clue: Woman resting chin on hand next to woman covering mouth with veil/mantilla; one invitingly lifts her veil while bespectacled woman looks on; boy's butt exposed to viewer
examples: showed a woman resting her chin on her hand next to a woman covering her mouth with her veil in his painting Two Women at a Window
a leering woman invitingly lifts her veil next to a stone-cold somber bespectacled woman holding the head of a boy whose butt is exposed to the viewer

clues.clue: Depicts Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid
examples: A flirtatious woman at a window covers her laugh with her mantilla shawl in this artist's painting A Girl and Her Duena, one of his many depictions of Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid

works.name: Penitent Magdalene
works.description: Murillo's version of the Penitent Magdalene features a skull and a cross sitting on a ledge to the right, with the main subject clad in red, looking upward and to the left. Other well-known versions of this scene were painted by Caravaggio, Titian, and Georges de la Tour.

clues.clue: Skull and cross on ledge to the right; subject clad in red looking upward and to the left
examples: Bartolome Murillo's version of this scene features a skull and a cross sitting on a ledge to the right and sees the main subject clad in red looking upwards and to the left

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Spanish Baroque painter from Seville. Contemporary of Velazquez. Cultivated 'cool,' 'warm,' and 'vaporous' (estilo vaporoso) styles depending on degree of tenebrism. Early career influenced by Zurbaran (the 'Spanish Caravaggio'). Died after falling from scaffolding at a church in Cadiz while painting Mystic Marriage of St. Catherine. Painted St. Isidore of Seville (patron saint of the Internet) for Seville Cathedral.

comprehensive_summary:
Bartolome Esteban Murillo (1617-1682) was a Spanish Baroque painter from Seville, where he spent most of his career. He was a contemporary of Velazquez. His paintings are categorized into three styles based on degree of tenebrism: 'cool,' 'warm,' and 'vaporous' (estilo vaporoso), with the vaporous style representing his mature work with soft, diffuse tones.

He is best known for his nearly twenty versions of the Immaculate Conception, which feature Mary in a blue cloak surrounded by cherubs, with putti blending into the background like haze. In many versions the central figure stands on a crescent moon surrounded by cherubs. The Immaculate Conception of Los Venerables was possibly the most expensive painting in the world when the Louvre purchased it from Marshal Soult's estate. 'The Colossal' version unusually features a full moon at the bottom with cherubs struggling to hold up a cloud. Unlike other painters' versions, his omit references to the Marian litanies. In one version, Mary stands atop a pile of angels.

His genre scenes of the street children and beggars of Seville are equally famous. The Young Beggar and Boys Eating Grapes and Melon depict poor children in vivid detail. Early tenebrist paintings include The Young Beggar and Girl of Galicia. Two Women at a Window (A Girl and Her Duena) shows a flirtatious woman lifting her veil (or hiding her snicker beneath her shawl) next to a somber bespectacled duena holding a boy's head, depicting Galician-born prostitutes in Madrid.

Murillo's early career was influenced by Zurbaran (the 'Spanish Caravaggio'), and like Velazquez, he lightened his use of tenebrism later in his career. He was also later influenced by Velazquez, the leading light of Philip IV's court. He created the most widely circulated portrait of St. Isidore of Seville (patron saint of the Internet) for Seville Cathedral. Other religious works include Vision of St. Anthony (man prostrate on knee, hands out at throng of angels), Saints Justa and Rufina, Young Man with a Basket of Fruit, the unfinished Heavenly and Earthly Trinities, and a Penitent Magdalene featuring a skull and cross on a ledge with the saint clad in red looking upward and to the left. A portrait attributed to him features concentric circles representing the heliocentric model, linked to the Galileo legend of 'Eppur si muove.' He died after falling from scaffolding while painting the Mystic Marriage of St. Catherine for a Capuchin church in Cadiz.
